authentication_flow.description: Create a Kraken Token (JWT) for authentication

input_fields.name: email
input_fields.description: Email address of the account user. Use with 'password' field.

input_fields.name: password
input_fields.description: Password of the account user. Use with 'email' field.

input_fields.name: APIKey
input_fields.description: API key of the account user. Use standalone, don't provide a second input field.

input_fields.name: organizationSecretKey
input_fields.description: Live secret key of an third-party organization. Use standalone.

input_fields.name: preSignedKey
input_fields.description: Short-lived, temporary key (that's pre-signed). Use standalone.

input_fields.name: refreshToken
input_fields.description: The refresh token that can be used to extend the expiry claim of a Kraken token.

input_fields.name: captchaResponse
input_fields.description: The response from the CAPTCHA challenge. Use with 'email' and 'password' fields.

method_2_api_key.description: Login with API key from user profile

method_3_refresh.note: Use to extend session without re-auth

example_mutation.with_api_key: mutation { obtainKrakenToken(input: { APIKey: "YOUR_API_KEY" }) { token refreshToken payload } }
example_mutation.with_credentials: mutation { obtainKrakenToken(input: { email: "user@example.com", password: "your_password" }) { token refreshToken payload } }

home_assistant_recommendation.reason: API keys are stable, don't expire frequently, and are designed for programmatic access
home_assistant_recommendation.storage: Store in Home Assistant secrets.yaml or integration config flow
home_assistant_recommendation.refresh_strategy: Use refreshToken to extend session, fallback to APIKey if refresh fails
